coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of homogeneous carbon quantum dots by ultrafast dual-beam pulsed laser ablation for bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, L.
Ren, X.
Wang, J.
Sun, M.
coredata.dc:description: Pursuit of an ultrafast and highly efficient method to fabricate homogeneous carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with high quantum yield is a persistent objective for photoluminescence (PL) mechanism and bioimaging. Here, the CQDs were fabricated through ablation of low-cost carbon cloth by ultrafast and highly efficient dual-beam pulsed laser ablation. Unlike previous reports, a single laser beam is divided into double beams by a beam splitter in this work for shortening laser ablation time and improving ablation yield. More importantly, the obtained CQDs are more homogeneous than those obtained by single-beam pulsed laser ablation, and the corresponding quantum yield of emission is as high as 35.4%. The homogeneous CQDs show a stable and excitation-independent PL emission, which is attributed to the electron transition from the π∗ state to the surface state rather than from the π∗ state to the π state based on our experimental evidence. Furthermore, the CQDs have a good stability and excellent antijamming performance, which are well suitable for cell bioimaging. Therefore, a ‘dual-beam pulsed ablation’ technique proposed as an ultrafast method is used to fabricate homogeneous CQDs with high quantum yield for the study of PL emission mechanism and cell bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Laser chemistry
Imaging agents
PL emission mechanism
Photochemistry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation process to synthesize CQDs by dual-beam pulsed laser ablation. The inset shows an enlarged image of a carbon cloth in the solvent ablated by a dual-beam pulsed laser. CQD = carbon quantum dot.
(a) SEM, (b) enlarged SEM, and (c)–(d) mapping and EDS images of a carbon cloth. SEM = scanning electron microscopy; EDS = Energy dispersive spectrometer.

Morphology characterization of DMSO-CQDs prepared by dual-beam pulsed laser ablation and single-beam pulsed laser ablation. (a) TEM and (b) enlarged TEM images of the DMSO-CQDs obtained by dual-beam pulsed laser ablation. (d) TEM and (e) enlarged TEM images of the DMSO-CQDs obtained by single-beam pulsed laser ablation. The inset is HRTEM images of the corresponding DMSO-CQDs. (c and f) Statistic size distributions of the DMSO-CQDs determined from TEM images (a) and (d). TEM = transmission electron microscopy; HRTEM = high-resolution transmission electron microscopy; DMSO = dimethylsulfoxide; CQD = carbon quantum dot.

(a) AFM image of the DMSO-CQDs obtained by dual-beam pulsed laser ablation. (b) Height distribution profile of DMSO-CQDs along line 1 and line 2 marked in Fig. S2(a). (c) Statistical analysis of the heights of DMSO-CQDs determined by AFM. AFM = Atomic Force Microscope; DMSO = dimethylsulfoxide; CQD = carbon quantum dot.

(a) Raman spectrum, (b) XPS spectrum, (c) High-resolution C1s spectrum, and (d) FTIR spectrum of CQDs synthesized by double-beam pulsed laser ablation in DMSO, the expanded plot of the doublet peaks at 1704 and 1634 cm−1. DMSO = dimethylsulfoxide; CQD = carbon quantum dot; XPS = X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy.
Schematic diagram of mechanism of DMSO-CQD domains passivated by DMSO molecules. DMSO = dimethylsulfoxide; CQD = carbon quantum dot.

Optical properties of CQDs synthesized in different solvents. UV-vis absorption and PL spectra of CQDs synthesized in (a) DMSO and (b) EA. Inset images show the photographs of DMSO-CQDs and EA-CQDs under a room light and an UV light source, respectively. The expanded plot of the absorption peak at 372 nm for EA-CQDs. (c) PLE spectra of CQDs synthesized in DMSO and EA solvents, respectively. PL spectra of CQDs excited with different excitation wavelengths in (d) DMSO and (e) EA. (f) Schematic diagram of electronic transitions of the CQDs. DMSO = dimethylsulfoxide; EA = ethyl acetate; CQD = carbon quantum dot; PLE = photoluminescence excitation; PL = photoluminescence.
(a) PL emission spectra of DMSO-CQDs at different temperatures. (b) Temperature dependence of DMSO-CQDs. PL = photoluminescence; DMSO = dimethylsulfoxide; CQD = carbon quantum dot.

(a) PL intensities of DMSO-CQDs after adding various concentrations of NaCl (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 100 mM) in PBS (pH 7.4). (b) PL intensities of DMSO-CQDs at different pH values from 4 to 9. (c) PL intensities of DMSO-CQDs at an excitation wavelength at 380 nm after 3600 s. PL = photoluminescence; DMSO = dimethylsulfoxide; CQD = carbon quantum dot; PBS = phosphate buffer saline.

MTT assay of viability of live HeLa cells after incubation of 24 h with different concentrations of DMSO-CQDs. MTT = 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromide; DMSO = dimethylsulfoxide; CQD = carbon quantum dot.

Bright-field microphotographs, confocal fluorescence microphotographs, and overlay microphotographs of live HeLa cells after incubation (a–c) without DMSO-CQDs and (d–f) with 200 μg/mL of DMSO-CQDs excited at 405 nm. DMSO = dimethylsulfoxide; CQD = carbon quantum dot.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of homogeneous carbon quantum dots by ultrafast dual-beam pulsed laser ablation for bioimaging

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Computational Center for Property and Modification on Nanomaterials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Computational Center for Property and Modification on Nanomaterials, College of Sciences, Liaoning Shihua University, Fushun, 113001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Pursuit of an ultrafast and highly efficient method to fabricate homogeneous carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with high quantum yield is a persistent objective for photoluminescence (PL) mechanism and bioimaging. Here, the CQDs were fabricated through ablation of low-cost carbon cloth by ultrafast and highly efficient dual-beam pulsed laser ablation. Unlike previous reports, a single laser beam is divided into double beams by a beam splitter in this work for shortening laser ablation time and improving ablation yield. More importantly, the obtained CQDs are more homogeneous than those obtained by single-beam pulsed laser ablation, and the corresponding quantum yield of emission is as high as 35.4%. The homogeneous CQDs show a stable and excitation-independent PL emission, which is attributed to the electron transition from the π∗ state to the surface state rather than from the π∗ state to the π state based on our experimental evidence. Furthermore, the CQDs have a good stability and excellent antijamming performance, which are well suitable for cell bioimaging. Therefore, a ‘dual-beam pulsed ablation’ technique proposed as an ultrafast method is used to fabricate homogeneous CQDs with high quantum yield for the study of PL emission mechanism and cell bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs), zero-dimension carbon-based nanomaterials with a hexagonal structure, have attracted increasing interests in recent years owing to their unique structure and outstanding properties [1–3]. As a potential competitor to organic dyes or conventional semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs present many remarkable advantages, such as low cost [4], stable chemical inertness [5], low cell toxicity [6], good biocompatibility, and fascinating optical properties [7–9], and thus, they are considered as promising candidates for various applications, including photocatalysis [10], biological sensing [11], drug delivery [12], photodynamic therapy [13,14], light-emitting devices, and solar cells [15,16].

To date, CQDs have been conventionally fabricated by various methods, including hydrothermal routes [17,18], microwave-assisted methods [19], electrochemical strategies [20], acidic exfoliation [21], and so on. Most of these synthesis methods are usually relatively time-consuming, tedious, and environmentally hazardous. Some toxic chemical reagents are also introduced in preparation processes of CQDs, which limits their applications. In addition, there are still some other disadvantages in these methods, such as strongly acidic conditions and high temperature, which can result in some impurities, confusing the photoluminescence (PL) mechanism of the CQD. Thus, the understanding of PL mechanism of CQDs is still unambiguous. The PL emission of CQDs is sometimes attributed to intrinsic state electron transition (from the π∗ to π state) [1,21,22] and sometimes attributed to the edge-state electron transition (from the π∗ to the surface state) [23–25].

The pulsed laser ablation (PLA), as a unique and promising approach, has been applied in the preparation of nanomaterials especially CQDs owing to advantages such as less time-consuming nature and simple operation [26–29]. During laser ablation process, the local high pressure and high temperature can directly cut a target into microparticles/nanoparticles without residues, avoiding introduction of impurities and eliminating contamination. Recently, several studies have reported the effects of laser parameters on CQDs, such as laser energy, laser pulse width, ablation time [27,28], and so on. In addition, the effect of different solvents on PL emission of CQDs has also been studied [29–31]. Thus, PL emission of the obtained CQDs by PLA has been intensively studied and mostly presents an excitation-dependent feature [10,20,32]. However, conventional PLA is still less efficient, and the obtained CQDs are inhomogeneous in shape and size, impeding their further applications [33]. Moreover, PL quantum yields of the reported CQDs are still relatively low (often less than 20%) by PLA [28,29,33,38]. The research on CQDs has entered a new era to date. Hence, for practical applications, it is still a challenge to fabricate homogeneous CQDs with high quantum yield using an ultrafast method.

In this work, CQDs have been fabricated by using a homemade dual-beam PLA system from low-cost carbon cloth. A single-laser beam is divided into two laser beams for shortening laser ablation time and improving the yield in the preparation process of CQDs. Here, the quantum yield of CQDs is as high as 35.4%. The obtained CQDs are homogeneous and present a stable and excitation-independent PL emission, which is attributed to the electron transition from the π∗ state to the surface state. In addition, CQDs with good stability and excellent antijamming performance show excellent bioimaging ability. Therefore, we propose an ultrafast preparation method to fabricate homogeneous CQDs with high quantum yield for the study of PL emission mechanism and cell bioimaging.

CQDs were fabricated by dual-beam laser cutting a low-cost carbon cloth (model: wos1009; Ce Tech supplies) in dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) and ethyl acetate (EA) (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd), which were given the name DMSO-CQDs and EA-CQDs, respectively. PLA was carried out by using a Q-switched Nd:YAG laser (10 Hz, 1064 nm, a pulse width of 6 ns and a pulse energy of 20 mJ, respectively). The ablation time is 10 min. A schematic illustration of the dual-beam PLA setup was given by 3D Max software (3ds Max 2015), which is shown in Fig. 1 . After that, the yellowish solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm.

Surface morphology and element analysis of the carbon cloth were obtained using the field emission scanning electron microscope (model: JSM-7500 F, Japan). Tranmission electron microsopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the CQDs were taken using a JEM-2100F microscope. The CQD solution was directly dropped onto a 200-mesh copper grid coated with an ultrathin carbon support film and dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 12 h for TEM measurement. Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) images of the CDQs were attained on a Park System XE-100 microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was carried out using an ESCA lab 250 spectrometer. The Raman spectrum was recorded by Renishaw spectroscopy excited at 532 nm (1.0 mW) and 1800 lines per millimeter grating at room temperature. The CQD solution was directly dropped on Si/SiO2 substrate (2 cm × 2 cm) and dried in air for 24 h for AFM, XPS, and Raman measurement. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded on the Nicolet IS5 spectrophotometer. UV-vis absorption experiments were performed by using a TU-1901 spectrometer. PL emission and PL excitation (PLE) spectra were measured using an F-7000 fluorescent spectrophotometer. PL quantum yield was recorded on the Edinburgh FLS 1000 spectrometer with an integrating sphere. The bioimaging images of HeLa cells were obtained on an Olympus FV1000 confocal laser scanning microscope excited at 405 nm, and the fluorescence wavelengths were recorded in the ranges of 400–500 nm.

The viability of cells was assessed by using an 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. HeLa cells were incubated with different concentrations of DMSO-CQDs. The culture media were removed and washed with phosphate buffer saline (pH 7.4) after 24 h. Subsequently, MTT solution was added to wells. After 3 h, MTT was replaced with DMSO. Each experiment was performed five times.

HeLa cells (human cervical cancer cells) were singled out for cell imaging of DMSO-CQDs. First, the HeLa cells were seeded in sterile culture dishes containing 2 mL of Dulbecco’s modified Eagle medium (DMEM) (penicillin and streptomycin, 1.5 g/L of sodium bicarbonate, 10% fetal bovine serum) for allowing the cells to adhere to the plate at constant temperature, and the plates were incubated in a humidified incubator under 5% CO2 for 1 day. Then, DMEM was replaced with the medium (0.15 mg/mL of DMEM) containing 200 μg/mL of CQDs, and the resultant was further incubated at 37 °C for 3 h. Another batch of HeLa cells, as a control experiment, was incubated without CQDs under the same conditions. Moreover, the culture medium was removed, and the cells were washed with PBS (0.01 M, pH = 7.4) for five times. Each sample was performed in duplicate.
The surface morphology and element analysis of the carbon cloth were obtained using the field emission scanning electron microscope, which can be seen in Fig. 2 .

Fig. 3 (a) and (b) show TEM images of the DMSO-CQDs fabricated by dual-beam PLA from a carbon cloth target, indicating these particles are homogeneous and monodispersed. The HRTEM image of DMSO-CQDs in the inset of Fig. 3(b) reveals crystallinity with lattices fringes of 0.21 nm, which are ascribed to the (0–110) plane of the graphite crystal [17]. Statistically, a lateral size distribution of the DMSO-CQD is 2–6 nm, and most of these particles are approximately 3.8 nm in size, as shown in Fig. 3(c). Subsequently, a comparing experiment was also carried out by single-beam PLA. As shown in Fig. 3(d)–(f), the obtained CQDs by single-beam PLA are inhomogeneous, and the yield is relatively low, which is in good agreement with that obtained in the previous reports [27,28].

The topography of the DMSO-CQDs is shown in Fig. 4 (a), and the measured heights along line 1 and line 2 are given in Fig. 4(b). The average height of the DMSO-CQDs is approximately 2 nm, as shown in Fig. 4(c), which indicates that the CQDs have a few-layered structure. In addition, TEM images of EA-CQDs are shown in Fig. S1, which are similar to the morphology characterization of DMSO-CQDs, indicating that the solvent does not affect the size of CQDs.

First, in Raman spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 5 (a), which is considered a distinct fingerprint, the DMSO-CQDs have three characteristic Raman peaks centered at 1345 cm−1 (D band), 1560 cm−1 (G band), and 2687 cm−1 (2D), respectively. The Raman intensity ratio of ID/IG is 0.86, indicating that more defects were formed on the periphery of the DMSO-CQDs [34]. In addition, the presence of 2D suggests that the DMSO-CQDs possess a few-layered structure, which is in good agreement with that of the previous AFM result. To further confirm the chemical constituents of the DMSO-CQDs, XPS was performed, and the spectrum shows three predominant peaks, as shown in Fig. 5(b), which are located at 159.2 eV (S2p), 284.8 eV (C1s peak), and 532.8 eV (O1s), respectively [35]. The corresponding high-resolution energy spectrum of the C1s peak, shown in Fig. 5(c), shows the presence of oxygen-containing groups (C–O at 287.2 eV and C=O at 288.7 eV) and C–S bonds (286.2 eV). Moreover, the FTIR spectrum of DMSO-CQDs is shown in Fig. 5(d). The strong absorption band at around 1063 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibrations of S=O. The two overlapped bands centered at 1704 cm−1 and 1634 cm−1 (as shown in the expanded plot in Fig. 5(d)) correspond to the stretching vibrations of C=O and C=C, respectively [35]. The wide band within the range of 3220–3607 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H. The absorption bands at 952 cm−1 and 661 cm−1 are the stretching vibrations of C–O–C and C–S, respectively [34,35].

In addition, the same spectrum characterizations of EA-CQDs are also shown in Fig. S2. Comparing with XPS and FTIR spectra of DMSO-CQDs, the CQDs passivated by EA still retain a hexagonal structure, as shown in Fig. S2(a), and do not possess sulfur-containing groups, whereas some other functional group such as COO− can be observed in Fig. S2(b)–(d). All these suggest that DMSO-CQDs and EA-CQDs have different functional structures from different solvents on the periphery of CQDs.

Here, a brief interpretation about the formation process of DMSO-CQDs by dual-beam PLA is presented, which is shown in Fig. 6 . Coulomb explosion occurs, and high thermionic electron emission produces a plasma plume with high temperature, high pressure, and extreme high intensity of local electromagnetic fields when two orthogonal laser beam pulses simultaneously irradiate the carbon cloth target. The microzone of the carbon cloth is cracked to form the sp2 carbon domain owing to absorption of high enough energy. Simultaneously, DMSO molecules adjacent to the carbon cloth surface are also pyrolyzed by the high temperature to form oxygen-containing and sulfur-containing substituents. At this time, C atoms with high surface energy at the periphery of the sp2 carbon domain rapidly combine with pyrolyzed substituents. Thus, the sp2 nanosized domain modified by abundant substituents forms CQDs with a passivation layer. In addition, for dual-beam PLA, the second laser beam increases ablation probability of nanoparticles simultaneously cut by the first laser beam at the same domain for obtaining more homogeneous CQDs. Hence, the dual-beam PLA technique has much higher ablation efficiency than the single-beam PLA for preparation of CQDs. More importantly, the obtained CQDs are very homogeneous.

As shown in Fig. 7 (a), two absorption peaks are clearly observed in the UV-vis spectrum of DMSO-CQDs. The shorter wavelength absorption peak at 276 nm is attributed to the π-π∗ transition of the sp2 nanosized domain [12,34]. The longer wavelength absorption peak at 389 nm is ascribed to the surface state-to-π∗ state transition [34,36]. The maximum emission peak centered at 448 nm with a full width at half maximum of 81 nm is observed in the PL spectrum. The inset in Fig. 7(a) shows an optical image of DMSO-CQDs irradiated by an UV lamp (λ = 365 nm), which indicates these particles can produce a bright indigo blue PL emission. In addition, different from the previous CQDs obtained by single-beam PLA, PL emission peaks of DMSO-CQDs obtained by dual-beam PLA remain unshifted with progressively longer excitation wavelengths, as shown in Fig. 7(b). And PL intensities of the DMSO-CQDs increase first and then decrease, with the excitation wavelengths changing from 330 nm to 390 nm. The maximum excitation is approximately 380 nm. The PLE spectrum was measured by monitoring the PL emission peak of 448 nm, showing an optimum excitation peak at 383 nm with a full width at half maximum of 73 nm, as shown in Fig. 7(c). PL emissions of DMSO and EA-CQDs also show an excitation wavelength–independent characteristic, as shown in Fig. 7(d) and (e), whereas the maximum PL peak position has a blue shift of 12 nm in EA.

To date, PL mechanism of the CQD is still a matter of current debate among researchers despite extensive studies being conducted [37]. As mentioned previously, the PL emission of CQDs is unambiguous, which is sometimes attributed to intrinsic state transition (from π∗ to π states) and sometimes attributed to the surface state transition (from the π∗ to the surface states). Here, we give a rational explanation for the PL mechanism of homogeneous CQDs. As shown in Fig. 7(f), owing to passivation of the pyrolyzed substituents on the periphery of CQDs, the surface states are created between the π and π∗ states. These pyrolyzed substituents can donate their electrons to the sp2 hybrid domains, resulting in an increase in the charge density of CQDs. The energy levels of surface states of the CQDs are raised, and the bandwidth becomes narrow. The bandwidth remains unchanged until CQDs are sufficiently passivated by the pyrolyzed substituents to attain a saturated value and is insensitive to the size of CQDs, showing a stable and excitation wavelength–independent feature in the indigo blue region.

As additional evidence, the UV-vis spectrum, PL, and PLE spectra of EA-CQDs are also shown in Fig. 7(b) and (c). PL emissions of DMSO and EA-CQDs also show an excitation wavelength–independent characteristic in Fig. 7(d) and (e), whereas the maximum PL peak position has a blue shift of 12 nm in EA. Actually, the structures of CQDs are mainly composed of sp2 domains and passivation groups. The sp2 domains of DMSO-CQDs and EA-CQDs are almost the same in size. However, the lengths of the chains of the passivated groups are different, the shorter the molecular chains, the smaller the sizes of CQDs, which causes the lower energy levels of surface states [38]. The bandgaps of the DMSO-CQDs and the EA-CQDs are close at saturation states. Therefore, there is a slight blue shift in PL emission for EA-CQDs, which further indicates that the stable and excitation wavelength–independent PL emission originates from electron transition from the π∗ to surface states rather than from π∗ to π states, which is conventionally assigned.

In addition, the intensity of PL emission of DMSO-CQDs also remains constant roughly from 80 K to 300 K, which is shown in Fig. 8 . The PL intensities of the DMSO-CQDs are insensitive to the temperature, indicating that indigo blue PL emission originates from surface states [39–41]. Finally, the quantum yield of the obtained CQDs is as high as 35.4%, which exceeds that of CQDs obtained by single-beam PLA in previous reports [28,31,35,38].

For practical applications in bioimaging, the antijamming performance and stability of CQDs under different severe conditions are very significant. First, PL intensities of DMSO-CQDs with increasing concentration of NaCl were measured, which shows excellent antijamming ability even at high saline concentrations (up to 100 mM NaCl) in Fig. 9 (a). In addition, PL intensities of DMSO-CQDs are relatively stable within a wide range of pH values (pH 4–9), as shown in Fig. 9(b), which further demonstrates that the obtained DMSO-CQDs have a good acid and base resistance property [13]. Moreover, the photostability measurement of DMSO-CQDs was performed for assessing the light fastness of the CQDs. As shown in Fig. 9(c), PL intensities of DMSO-CQDs are almost unchanged even when excited at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm for 3600 s, indicating that DMSO-CQDs have excellent photostability for 3600 s. Therefore, DMSO-CQDs with a stable structure and stable PL property, as a practical staining material, are well suitable for cell bioimaging.

To evaluate the feasibility of the CQDs as a stable staining biomaterial, HeLa cells were selected to assess the property of the DMSO-CQDs for bioimaging. First, the inherent cytotoxicity of the DMSO-CQDs with live HeLa cells under different concentrations through the MTT viability assay was assessed. The results indicate that DMSO-CQDs have very low toxicity to live HeLa cells, even when their concentrations are approximately 1000 μg/mL (see Fig. 10).

And then, the DMSO-CQDs were added in 12-well dishes and incubated with HeLa cells. The confocal images of HeLa cells incubated in the absence of DMSO-CQDs are shown in Fig. 11 (a)–(c); it is found that there is no fluorescence in HeLa cells. Obviously, Fig. 11(d)–(f) shows high contrast confocal microscopy images of HeLa cells incubated with DMSO-CQDs. Compared with the confocal images of HeLa cells incubated in the absence of DMSO-CQDs, as shown in Fig. 11(a)–(c), HeLa cells incubated with the DMSO-CQDs present a bright blue PL emission (Fig. 11(e) and (f)), which shows that DMSO-CQDs have been internalized by HeLa cells and have good biocompatibility, indicating that DMSO-CQDs could be taken as a sort of efficient staining material for cell bioimaging.

In summary, CQDs have been fabricated through ablation of low-cost carbon cloth with the orthogonal pulsed laser double-beam for shortening laser ablation time and improving the yield. More importantly, the CQDs are more homogeneous, and the corresponding quantum yield of emission is as high as 35.4%, which exceeds that of CQDs obtained by single-beam PLA. Here, the obtained CQDs passivated by organic molecules show a stable and excitation wavelength–independent PL emission, which is attributed to the electrons transition from the π∗ state to the surface state rather than from the π∗ state to the π state based on our experimental evidence. The PL peak remains unshifted with increase in excitation wavelengths owing to sufficient passivation effect. In addition, the CQDs with good stability and excellent antijamming performance are applied in HeLa cell bioimaging and show good biocompatibility. Therefore, we propose an ultrafast double-beam ablation technique to prepare homogeneous CQDs with high quantum yield for the study of PL mechanism and cell bioimaging.

Lin Cui: Data curation and Formal analysis, Roles/Writing – original draft; Writing – review & editing; Xin Ren: Data curation and Formal analysis, Roles/Writing – original draft; Writing – review & editing; Jingang Wang: Roles/Writing – original draft; Writing – review & editing; Mengtao Sun: Data curation and Formal analysis, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Roles/Writing – original draft; Writing – review & editing

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (grant nos. 91436102, 11374353, and 11874084), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, and the NSFC-BRICS STI Framework Program (no. 51861145309).
